short_description: This test measures alkaline phosphatase (ALP), an enzyme found in various tissues. Elevated levels can suggest issues affecting either the liver or bones.
clinical_overview: Alkaline phosphatase is an enzyme that exists in several body tissues, including the bone and the liver. Measuring its level in the blood helps healthcare providers evaluate how well these organs are functioning. High results may indicate increased activity due to conditions such as certain types of bone disease or bile duct issues within the liver. Conversely, low levels can also point toward underlying health concerns. These results are used by doctors alongside your symptoms and physical exam findings to help determine a possible cause for abnormal enzyme levels.

short_description: This test measures blood glucose levels at multiple time points to see how your body processes sugar after consuming a standardized amount of sugar.
clinical_overview: The Glucose Tolerance Test (GTT) evaluates how well the body manages blood sugar (glucose). During this procedure, you consume a measured sugary drink, and blood samples are taken at specific intervals. These measurements help determine if the pancreas and body tissues are effectively processing glucose after a dietary challenge. Elevated levels of blood sugar over time may suggest impaired glucose tolerance or diabetes mellitus. The results are used by healthcare providers to assess your risk for metabolic issues and guide management strategies.

short_description: This test measures the amount of antibodies in your blood that target a protein called antitrypsin. Levels can help doctors understand immune system activity or specific conditions.
clinical_overview: The Antitrypsin Quantitative assay measures the concentration of antibodies in the blood that are directed against the protein antitrypsin. This protein is naturally found in the body and plays roles in protecting tissues, particularly in the lungs and liver. Doctors order this test to evaluate potential immune system responses or when specific conditions affecting these proteins are suspected. Abnormal levels may suggest that the immune system is reacting unusually to the protein or related substances. The results help guide further testing but cannot diagnose a condition on their own.

short_description: This test measures Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), a protein found in the blood. Elevated levels may indicate liver issues or certain types of cancer.
clinical_overview: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is a protein marker that can be measured in the blood. While AFP is naturally produced by the liver and yolk sac during fetal development, high levels in adults are often ordered to help evaluate suspected liver disease or specific cancers, such as hepatocellular carcinoma. The test helps doctors monitor whether cancer is present or if it is responding to treatment. It is important to understand that elevated AFP alone does not confirm a diagnosis; results must always be interpreted by a healthcare provider alongside your symptoms and physical exam findings.

short_description: This test measures a specific marker in sunflower seeds to check for contamination or quality issues, ensuring food safety.
clinical_overview: This test is designed to detect a particular substance or contaminant within sunflower seed samples. It is used primarily for quality control and assessing the safety of agricultural products intended for consumption. The results indicate whether the tested batch contains acceptable levels of the marker or if contamination is present. A positive result suggests that the seeds may require further investigation, while a negative result generally indicates the product meets established safety standards.

short_description: This test detects genetic material from Common Ragweed, which is an airborne allergen. It determines if ragweed allergens are present in the sample.
clinical_overview: This test measures the presence of specific DNA markers belonging to Common Ragweed pollen. These markers help determine if a patient has been exposed to this particular plant allergen in the tested environment or sample. The test is used by healthcare providers when symptoms, such as sneezing, itchy eyes, or respiratory issues, suggest seasonal allergies or asthma triggered by airborne pollens. A positive result indicates that ragweed allergens were detected; however, it does not confirm a diagnosis of allergy or asthma on its own. These results help guide treatment planning and environmental management.

short_description: This test measures specific components related to dandelion in your body. It helps evaluate potential imbalances or markers that may be associated with certain health conditions.
clinical_overview: The IMCAP DANDELION (W8) test is designed to measure the presence and level of particular biological markers, which are sometimes linked to compounds found in dandelions. Clinicians typically order this test when evaluating a patient's overall metabolic profile or investigating symptoms that suggest potential imbalances in detoxification pathways. The results provide information about these specific markers but do not diagnose any single condition on their own. Any findings must be interpreted by your healthcare provider alongside your medical history, physical exam, and other laboratory tests.

short_description: This test measures specific chemical markers found in Russian Thistle plant material to confirm its identity and quality.
clinical_overview: This analysis is designed to measure the presence and concentration of unique chemical compounds within Russian Thistle plant samples. It does not assess human health or diagnose medical conditions. The test is typically ordered for research, quality control, or verification purposes when confirming the source material used in herbal preparations. Results indicate whether the sample contains the expected markers at appropriate levels, which helps confirm the identity and purity of the thistle material being analyzed.

short_description: This test detects the specific genetic material (DNA) belonging to Rough Pigweed in a sample. It confirms whether this particular plant species is present.
clinical_overview: This molecular assay measures the unique DNA sequence found within the Rough Pigweed plant. The test is used for diagnostic evaluation to confirm the presence or absence of this specific weed species in environmental, tissue, or other collected samples. A positive result indicates that genetic material matching Rough Pigweed was detected at the site of collection. Conversely, a negative result suggests that the target DNA was not found. This testing helps identify invasive plant populations and is used by agricultural specialists to monitor weed spread.

short_description: This test measures several specific biological markers (substances in the body) to help evaluate overall health and assess potential underlying conditions.
clinical_overview: The IMCAP RG MARSH ELDER panel is designed to measure multiple distinct biological components within a sample. These markers are used by healthcare providers to gain a comprehensive view of a patient’s physiological status, helping them determine if further investigation is needed. The test does not diagnose any single condition on its own; rather, the results are interpreted alongside your medical history, physical exam findings, and other lab tests. Elevated or low levels of these markers may indicate inflammation, organ stress, or imbalances that require clinical follow-up.

short_description: This test detects specific genetic markers found in Sheep Sorrel plant tissue. It is used to confirm the identity or source of the material being analyzed.
clinical_overview: The assay analyzes samples to detect the presence of specific genetic markers associated with Sheep Sorrel. This testing is typically ordered when laboratory professionals need to confirm the precise identity, origin, or type of plant material for research, environmental monitoring, or agricultural assessment. The results help healthcare providers monitor existing conditions and guide treatment plans for patients with impaired clotting ability.

short_description: This test measures specific components related to sesame seeds in your sample. It helps determine if these markers are present at levels that require further medical review.
clinical_overview: The IMCAP SESAME SEED (F10) test is designed to measure the presence and level of particular biomarkers associated with sesame seed exposure or metabolism within a biological fluid. Physicians typically order this test when evaluating potential sensitivities, allergies, or nutritional status related to consuming sesame seeds. The results help healthcare providers determine if the measured components are elevated or present in levels that suggest a need for dietary modification or further diagnostic testing. It is important to note that these results must be interpreted by your doctor alongside your medical history and physical examination findings.

short_description: This blood test measures specific immune markers that may show if your body has an allergic reaction to peanuts. It helps determine if your immune system recognizes peanuts as a potential allergen.
clinical_overview: This laboratory test analyzes blood samples to detect the presence of antibodies, which are proteins produced by the immune system in response to certain substances. Physicians order this test when there is suspicion that a person may have an allergic reaction to peanuts, especially if symptoms like hives or digestive issues are present. A positive result suggests your body has developed an immune response to peanuts; however, these results must be interpreted alongside your medical history and physical examination findings. Negative results do not guarantee safety but indicate no detectable immune response at the time of testing.

short_description: This test detects specific substances or markers within soybean material to confirm its quality and safety for intended use.
clinical_overview: This laboratory test measures the presence of particular substances or genetic markers within soybean samples. It is ordered to evaluate whether the soybeans meet established standards for purity, allergen content, or contamination levels. A positive result indicates that the specific substance being tested for has been detected in the sample; conversely, a negative result suggests the material does not contain that marker above the laboratory's detection limit. These results are used by food safety experts to confirm suitability and ensure compliance with regulatory guidelines.

short_description: This test examines your DNA for specific genetic markers related to the HAZELNUT gene area. It helps determine if certain variations in your genes are present.
clinical_overview: This molecular test analyzes a patient's DNA sample to detect specific genetic variations within the region associated with the HAZELNUT marker. Physicians order this test when they need to evaluate potential risks or confirm diagnoses for conditions known to be linked to these particular gene markers. The results indicate whether certain genetic changes are present or absent, which can help guide treatment planning and inform family risk assessment. It is important to understand that these findings provide information about genetic predisposition and should be interpreted by a healthcare provider alongside your full medical history.

short_description: This test measures specific biomarkers to assess metabolic function and potential risk factors. Results help healthcare providers evaluate overall health status.
clinical_overview: The IMCAP ALMOND (F20) test analyzes a panel of biomarkers to measure how certain biological processes are functioning within the body. Physicians typically order this test when they need a detailed assessment of an individual's metabolic profile or potential risk for specific conditions, often in conjunction with symptoms or physical exam findings. The results provide data that helps guide treatment decisions and determine if lifestyle changes or further testing are necessary; however, these values must be interpreted by a healthcare provider alongside your full medical history to understand their clinical meaning.

short_description: This test measures several markers using specialized techniques to detect patterns of genetic or protein changes in your blood.
clinical_overview: The IMCAP CRAB panel analyzes multiple biomarkers within a sample, typically blood, to look for specific molecular signatures. This type of testing is ordered by healthcare providers when they need to evaluate potential causes of symptoms or confirm the presence of certain conditions. The results help determine if particular genetic markers or protein patterns are present, which may guide further diagnosis and treatment planning. It is important to note that these test results provide information about specific biomarkers and should be interpreted by a physician alongside your medical history and physical examination findings.

short_description: This test measures a specific biomarker in your blood. Elevated levels may indicate certain conditions or changes in bodily function.
clinical_overview: This laboratory test quantifies a specific protein or substance (biomarker) found in the bloodstream. It is ordered by healthcare providers to help evaluate potential underlying health issues and assess how well various body systems are functioning. The results provide information about the concentration of this marker, which can be elevated or low depending on what it measures. Abnormal levels may suggest a need for further investigation into specific conditions, but they do not confirm a diagnosis on their own.

short_description: This test detects specific markers or potential contaminants found in coconut samples. It helps determine if the material contains substances that could pose a health risk.
clinical_overview: The IMPAC COCONUT (F36) test is designed to detect the presence of specific biological markers or contaminants within sample materials derived from coconuts. This testing is typically ordered when there is concern about potential contamination, such as bacterial growth or toxins, that might be present in the food source. A positive result indicates that the tested material contains the detected marker or contaminant; however, this finding requires clinical correlation with symptoms and history to determine if further action is needed. Negative results suggest that no detectable contaminants were found at the time of testing.

short_description: This test detects specific genetic material related to TUNA. It helps determine if an individual has been exposed to or infected with the condition associated with this marker.
clinical_overview: This laboratory test measures the presence of genetic material linked to TUNA, which is used to evaluate potential exposure or infection. Physicians order this assay when they need to confirm a diagnosis or assess the risk of transmission related to the specific condition. A positive result indicates that the target genetic material was detected in the sample; however, these results must be interpreted alongside the patient's symptoms and medical history. The test is designed to assist clinicians in making an informed diagnostic decision.

short_description: This test detects the DNA of *Salmonella*, a type of bacteria that causes foodborne illness. It helps confirm if an active infection is present in stool samples.
clinical_overview: This laboratory test measures the genetic material (DNA) belonging to *Salmonella* bacteria, which are common pathogens responsible for gastrointestinal infections. The test is ordered when a healthcare provider suspects that diarrhea, fever, or abdominal pain may be caused by an active bacterial infection. A positive result indicates that *Salmonella* DNA was detected in the sample, suggesting the presence of the bacteria. Conversely, a negative result means that no detectable *Salmonella* DNA was found at the time of testing. These results are used to help guide treatment decisions and confirm the source of symptoms.

short_description: This test measures direct bilirubin, a waste product from red blood cells that has been processed by the liver. It helps doctors assess how well your liver is functioning or if there are blockages in bile flow.
clinical_overview: Bilirubin is a yellowish substance created when old red blood cells break down. The direct fraction measures the portion of this waste product that has been chemically attached to protein by the liver, making it ready for removal into bile. This test is ordered primarily to evaluate overall liver function and to determine if there are blockages in the bile ducts (a condition called cholestasis). Elevated levels can indicate various issues, such as hepatitis or gallstones causing an obstruction. The results help healthcare providers pinpoint whether the problem lies with the liver itself or with the flow of bile out of the body.

short_description: This test measures the amount of alpha-lactalbumin protein in your blood. It helps doctors evaluate general metabolic or nutritional status.
clinical_overview: The alpha-lactalbumin protein is a substance found in the bloodstream that plays a role in various bodily functions, including metabolism and nutrient processing. This quantitative test measures the concentration level of this specific protein to help healthcare providers assess overall physiological function. Physicians may order this test when they need to evaluate potential nutritional deficiencies or metabolic imbalances. Abnormal results could indicate underlying conditions affecting how the body processes nutrients, but these findings require correlation with your medical history and physical exam for a complete understanding.

short_description: This test measures the level of Beta-lactoglobulin, a protein found in milk, within your blood. It is used to help determine if you may have a sensitivity or reaction to milk proteins.
clinical_overview: This test measures the concentration of Beta-lactoglobulin, which is a specific type of protein naturally present in milk. Physicians order this test when they need to evaluate potential sensitivities or immune reactions related to consuming dairy products. The results help determine if there are elevated levels of this protein that might suggest an adverse reaction to milk components. It is important to note that these findings must be interpreted by your healthcare provider alongside your symptoms and medical history, as the test alone cannot diagnose a condition.

short_description: This test measures specific antibodies (IgM) in your blood that indicate recent or current exposure to the Hepatitis A virus.
clinical_overview: This blood test detects IgM antibodies, which are proteins made by the immune system, specifically targeting the Hepatitis A virus. These antibodies help healthcare providers determine if a person has been recently exposed to or is currently infected with Hep A. While positive results suggest an active infection, they must be interpreted alongside your symptoms, medical history, and physical exam findings. The presence of IgM antibodies typically indicates that the body is mounting a new immune response against the virus. This test is used diagnostically to help manage care for acute Hepatitis A infections.

short_description: This test measures specific casein proteins in your blood that help assess the health and function of your digestive tract lining.
clinical_overview: The IMCAP Casein test measures levels of certain casein proteins circulating in the bloodstream. These proteins are part of the natural protective barrier found within the gut lining. Physicians typically order this test when a patient presents with symptoms suggesting poor nutrient absorption or gastrointestinal distress, such as chronic diarrhea or unexplained weight loss. Elevated or decreased protein levels may indicate compromised intestinal barrier function or malabsorption issues. The results help healthcare providers determine if further investigation into digestive health is needed.

short_description: This test measures specific proteins or antibodies in the blood that may react to gluten, a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye.
clinical_overview: This laboratory test analyzes components of the blood to detect markers associated with an immune reaction to gluten. It is typically ordered when a patient presents with symptoms suggestive of digestive distress or malabsorption, such as chronic diarrhea or abdominal pain. The results help evaluate whether there is evidence of an adverse reaction to gluten ingestion. A positive result suggests that specific proteins are present in the blood; however, these findings must be interpreted by a healthcare provider alongside your medical history and physical exam to determine their clinical significance.

short_description: This test measures specific antibodies in the blood that react to proteins found in lobster. High levels may suggest an allergy or sensitivity to consuming lobster.
clinical_overview: This laboratory test measures the amount of Immunoglobulin E (IgE) antibodies present in the blood that are specifically directed against proteins from lobster. It is ordered when a doctor needs to evaluate whether a person has an allergic reaction to eating lobster. Elevated levels of these specific antibodies suggest that the immune system may react strongly to the protein, which can potentially cause symptoms like hives or digestive upset upon exposure. However, positive results alone do not confirm an allergy; they are one piece of information that doctors use alongside your medical history and physical exam findings.

short_description: This test measures specific proteins found in pecans that might trigger an allergic response. It helps determine if your immune system reacts to this food.
clinical_overview: This laboratory test detects specific protein components within pecan nuts that may be recognized by the body as allergens. Doctors typically order this test when a patient presents with symptoms suggestive of a nut allergy, such as hives or digestive upset, and requires confirmation regarding pecans. A positive result suggests that your immune system may be reacting to proteins in the pecan nut. However, these results must always be interpreted alongside your medical history, physical exam findings, and other lab tests, as they cannot diagnose all types of allergies on their own.

short_description: This test measures bilirubin, a yellowish waste product created when red blood cells break down. High levels can indicate that the liver or bile ducts are having trouble processing this substance.
clinical_overview: Bilirubin is a natural yellow pigment produced by the body as old red blood cells are broken down. The liver normally processes and removes bilirubin from the bloodstream through bile. This test measures the total amount of bilirubin present in the blood, helping doctors evaluate how well the liver and bile ducts are functioning. Elevated levels may suggest that there is an issue with the liver itself or a blockage in the pathways where bile flows. These results help guide further testing to determine the cause of high bilirubin.

short_description: This test measures the percentage of transferrin, a blood protein, that is carrying iron. It helps determine how much usable iron is available in your bloodstream.
clinical_overview: This quantitative test assesses the saturation level of iron bound to transferrin, which is a major protein responsible for transporting iron throughout the body. Physicians order this test primarily to evaluate the body's overall iron status and help diagnose various types of anemia or potential iron overload. Low levels may suggest that the body is not receiving enough usable iron, while high levels can indicate other metabolic issues. The results are interpreted alongside your symptoms, medical history, and other blood tests to guide treatment decisions.

short_description: This blood test measures a protein marker called CA125. Elevated levels can indicate issues with reproductive organs or certain types of cancer, but require further evaluation.
clinical_overview: The CA125 test measures the concentration of Cancer Antigen 125, a specific protein found in the bloodstream. This blood test is typically ordered to help monitor changes or evaluate symptoms related to pelvic conditions, such as ovarian disease. While elevated levels can be associated with certain types of cancer or other reproductive issues like endometriosis, they are not sufficient on their own to diagnose a condition. A high reading suggests that further diagnostic testing and clinical evaluation are necessary to determine the cause.

short_description: This test measures IgG antibodies, which are immune proteins in your blood that target the Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV). It indicates if you have had a previous infection or vaccination with VZV.
clinical_overview: The Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV) IgG antibody test measures specific types of antibodies in the blood. These antibodies are produced by the immune system after exposure to VZV, which causes diseases such as chickenpox and shingles. Physicians order this test primarily to determine if a person has protective immunity against the virus through prior infection or vaccination. A positive result suggests that the body has encountered VZV before, while a negative result may indicate no previous contact with the virus. This information is used by healthcare providers to guide preventative care and treatment planning.

short_description: This test measures urea nitrogen levels in your blood. It helps doctors check how well your kidneys are filtering and removing waste products from your body.
clinical_overview: Urea nitrogen is a waste product that forms when protein is broken down, and it is normally filtered out of the blood by the kidneys. This test measures the concentration of this waste material to help evaluate kidney function. Doctors order this test to assess how efficiently the kidneys are clearing metabolic waste from the bloodstream, which can be helpful in monitoring chronic kidney disease or diagnosing acute injury. Elevated levels may suggest that the kidneys are struggling to filter waste products (a condition called uremia), while very low levels could relate to diet or hydration status. The results must always be interpreted alongside your medical history and other test findings.

short_description: This test measures average blood sugar levels over the past 2–3 months by checking for glucose attached to hemoglobin in red blood cells.
clinical_overview: Glycated Hemoglobin (HbA1c) measures the percentage of hemoglobin—a protein found in red blood cells—that has become coated with sugar. Because red blood cells live for several months, this test provides a reliable picture of average blood glucose levels over the preceding 2 to 3 months, rather than measuring current sugar levels. Physicians order this test primarily to monitor how well blood sugar is managed over time, which is crucial for diagnosing and managing diabetes or prediabetes. Elevated results suggest that blood sugar control has been poor recently, while normal results indicate better long-term glucose management.

short_description: This test measures the level of a specific protein, called CA27.29, found in your blood. Elevated levels may indicate changes related to certain types of cancer.
clinical_overview: CA27.29 is a type of tumor marker—a substance measured in the blood that can be associated with certain cancers. This test is typically ordered not for initial diagnosis, but rather to monitor how well a patient's condition is being managed or to check if cancer has returned after treatment. The laboratory measures the concentration of this protein using specialized blood analysis methods. While high levels of CA27.29 may suggest changes in disease status, these results must always be interpreted by a doctor alongside your symptoms, physical exam findings, and other test results.

short_description: This test measures the ratio of blood urea nitrogen to creatinine. It helps doctors evaluate kidney function and assess potential fluid imbalances.
clinical_overview: The BUN/CREAT Ratio is a calculation that compares two waste products in the blood: blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine. Both substances are filtered by the kidneys, so measuring their ratio provides insight into how well the kidneys are working. Doctors typically order this test to help evaluate kidney function or when they suspect dehydration or excess fluid retention. An abnormal ratio may suggest issues with kidney filtration or could indicate that the body's hydration status is affecting waste product levels.

short_description: This test detects antibodies, which are proteins your body makes in response to Hepatitis C virus exposure. A positive result suggests that you have been exposed to the virus at some point.
clinical_overview: The HEP C AB test measures the presence of specific antibodies against the Hepatitis C virus (HCV). This blood test is used by healthcare providers to determine if a person has been previously exposed to HCV, indicating whether the immune system has mounted an antibody response. A positive result means that your body has encountered the virus and created protective proteins; however, this test does not measure how much active virus is circulating in the blood or assess current liver damage. Therefore, results must be interpreted by a doctor alongside your medical history, symptoms, and other lab findings to determine the overall clinical picture.

short_description: This test measures how quickly red blood cells settle in a tube over time. A faster settling rate can suggest the body has increased levels of inflammation or infection.
clinical_overview: This test measures the Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR), which tracks the speed at which red blood cells fall to the bottom of a glass tube. Doctors order this test to help evaluate if there is general inflammation or tissue damage occurring in the body, such as those associated with autoimmune conditions like rheumatoid arthritis. An elevated rate suggests that inflammatory proteins are present and circulating in high amounts, but it does not identify the specific cause of the inflammation. Because many things can raise this rate, results must always be interpreted by a healthcare provider alongside your symptoms, physical exam findings, and other blood tests.

short_description: This test examines your urine for signs of infection, abnormal cells, and protein levels. A culture helps identify specific types of bacteria present in the sample.
clinical_overview: A comprehensive urinalysis evaluates multiple components within a urine sample to assess kidney and urinary tract health. The analysis includes checking chemical markers (like blood or protein), examining physical elements under a microscope for abnormal cells, and performing a bacterial culture. This testing is ordered when symptoms such as burning during urination or unusual frequency are present, helping doctors determine if an infection or other issue is causing the symptoms. Positive results may indicate conditions like a urinary tract infection (UTI) or kidney stones, requiring further medical attention to confirm the cause.

short_description: This test grows specific germs from your urine to identify the type of microbe that may be causing a urinary tract infection.
clinical_overview: The CULT(U) SPECIAL is used to grow and identify specific types of microbes, called pathogens, present in a urine sample. Doctors typically order this test when they suspect an infection within the urinary system, such as cystitis or pyelonephritis. By growing these organisms in the lab, healthcare providers can determine exactly which germ is causing symptoms like burning during urination or frequent urges to go. The results help guide your doctor in selecting the most effective antibiotic treatment for the specific pathogen found.

short_description: This test detects Shiga toxins, which are poisons released by certain strains of E. coli bacteria in stool samples. A positive result helps identify a specific cause of severe stomach illness.
clinical_overview: The Shiga Toxins E. Coli test measures the presence of Shiga toxins—poisons produced by specific types of *E. coli* bacteria—in a stool sample. This test is ordered when a patient presents with symptoms such as severe diarrhea, abdominal pain, or signs of kidney problems that suggest an infection. A positive result indicates that the illness may be caused by these toxin-producing bacteria. Finding these toxins helps healthcare providers confirm the diagnosis and manage care to prevent potential complications, such as Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS).

short_description: This test measures thyroglobulin, a protein naturally produced by the thyroid gland, to help doctors monitor your overall thyroid health.
clinical_overview: Thyroglobulin is a specific protein found in the blood that originates from the tissue of the thyroid gland. This panel is often ordered to evaluate current thyroid function or to monitor for any signs of recurrence after treatment for thyroid cancer. Elevated levels may suggest active thyroid disease or residual thyroid tissue, while low levels are typically expected if the thyroid gland has been surgically removed. It is important to note that these results must be interpreted by your healthcare provider alongside other tests, such as TSH and ultrasound findings.

short_description: This test measures the amount of calcium, a vital mineral found in your blood. Calcium is necessary for proper nerve signaling and muscle function throughout the body.
clinical_overview: The calcium level test measures the concentration of calcium ions in the blood serum. This mineral is essential because it helps nerves send signals and allows muscles to contract properly. Doctors order this test when they need to check a person's overall mineral balance or investigate symptoms such as muscle weakness, tingling sensations (paresthesia), or changes in kidney function. Abnormal results—either too high or too low—may indicate issues with the parathyroid glands, vitamin D metabolism, or bone health. The results help guide treatment decisions but cannot diagnose a specific condition on their own.

short_description: This test measures specific antibodies (IgG, IgA, and IgM) in the blood that indicate if your body has been exposed to the yeast *Candida albicans*.
clinical_overview: This laboratory test assesses the immune system's response by detecting various types of antibodies against *Candida albicans*, a common type of yeast. Antibodies are proteins made by the immune system when it detects foreign substances, and measuring IgG, IgA, and IgM helps clinicians determine if exposure was recent or occurred in the past. Physicians typically order this test to help evaluate suspected candidiasis infections, such as those affecting the mouth or skin. The presence and type of antibodies may suggest a history of exposure or an active immune response, but these results must be interpreted alongside your symptoms, medical history, and physical examination findings.

short_description: This test measures a specific biological marker that helps assess how certain bodily systems are functioning. Results assist healthcare providers in evaluating general physiological balance.
clinical_overview: This assay analyzes the level of a proprietary marker to help evaluate metabolic or functional status within the body. Physicians may order this test when they need additional information regarding overall physiological balance, often based on symptoms or physical exam findings. The results are interpreted by a doctor and can suggest whether certain bodily processes may be altered. Abnormal levels of this marker indicate that further investigation is needed, but these results cannot diagnose a specific condition alone.

short_description: This test measures a specific protein marker found in mucus. It helps determine if there is evidence of inflammation or infection in the respiratory tract.
clinical_overview: The IMCAP OVOMUCOID assay detects levels of a particular biomarker within mucosal secretions, such as those collected via swab. This testing is ordered to evaluate for localized inflammatory processes or specific infectious agents affecting the airways. Elevated results may suggest active inflammation or infection in the respiratory tract, but they do not confirm a diagnosis on their own. The findings are interpreted by a healthcare provider alongside your symptoms and physical examination to guide appropriate treatment.

short_description: This test measures specific components in your blood that help assess how certain bodily systems are functioning. It is used to provide information about metabolic pathways and overall health status.
clinical_overview: The IMPCAP PAPRIKA panel analyzes several markers found in the blood to evaluate various metabolic processes within the body. Physicians typically order this test when they need a detailed picture of how different internal systems, such as liver or kidney function, are working together. The results help guide diagnosis and determine if further testing is needed; for example, abnormal levels might indicate impaired nutrient processing or inflammation. It is important to note that these results must be interpreted by your healthcare provider alongside your symptoms and medical history.

short_description: This test measures specific substances found in pineapple to check for potential allergic reactions or sensitivities. A positive result suggests your body may react to components from this fruit.
clinical_overview: This laboratory test detects markers related to components within pineapple. It is typically ordered when a patient presents with symptoms such as skin rashes, digestive upset, or difficulty breathing after consuming the fruit. The results help determine if an allergic reaction or sensitivity exists, which can guide necessary dietary changes or treatment plans. A positive result indicates that your body may be reacting to these substances, but this test alone cannot diagnose all causes of symptoms.

short_description: This blood test measures antibodies that can indicate a current or past syphilis infection. It helps healthcare providers monitor for signs of this sexually transmitted condition.
clinical_overview: The VDRL test analyzes the serum portion of your blood to detect antibodies against cardiolipin, which are substances released into the bloodstream when there is tissue damage. This test is used primarily to screen for and monitor syphilis infection. Results are reported as titers, which indicate the level of these antibodies present in the blood. A positive result suggests exposure to the bacteria but does not confirm a diagnosis on its own; it must be interpreted by a doctor alongside your symptoms and medical history. If positive, further testing is needed to determine if treatment is required.

short_description: This test measures the amount of lead in your blood. It helps determine if you have been exposed to high levels of lead, often from environmental or occupational sources.
clinical_overview: The Lead level test quantifies the concentration of lead metal found in the bloodstream. This test is ordered when a person may have been exposed to elevated amounts of lead through environmental contact, such as old paint, contaminated dust, or specific occupations. Elevated levels suggest that the body has absorbed significant amounts of lead and require further medical evaluation. High blood lead concentrations can be toxic and are associated with various health concerns, particularly affecting the nervous system. The results help healthcare providers determine if protective measures or treatments are necessary to reduce the body's lead burden.

short_description: This test measures the amount of Vitamin B12 in your blood. It helps determine if your body has sufficient levels of this essential nutrient.
clinical_overview: Vitamin B12 is an important vitamin necessary for healthy nerve function and the proper formation of red blood cells. This quantitative test measures the concentration of B12 circulating in the blood to assess nutritional status. Physicians may order this test if symptoms suggest a deficiency, such as fatigue or numbness, or if there are concerns about poor nutrient absorption. Low levels can indicate dietary insufficiency or issues with how the body absorbs the vitamin. The results help guide treatment decisions regarding supplementation or addressing underlying causes.

short_description: This test measures the activity of Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH), an enzyme found in many body tissues. High levels can suggest damage or breakdown of cells in various organs.
clinical_overview: Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) is an enzyme that naturally exists in high concentrations within most types of cells, including those in the heart, liver, and muscles. This blood test measures the total activity of this enzyme to help evaluate potential tissue damage or cell breakdown. Elevated LDH levels indicate that many cells may be damaged or dying, but they do not pinpoint the exact cause. Because LDH is released by multiple organs, doctors use these results alongside a patient's symptoms, medical history, and physical exam findings to narrow down the possible source of the problem.

short_description: This test measures Sex Hormone Binding Globulin (SHBG), a protein in your blood that carries sex hormones like estrogen and testosterone. It helps doctors estimate how much of these hormones are available to your body's tissues.
clinical_overview: Sex Hormone Binding Globulin (SHBG) is a protein produced by the liver that circulates in the bloodstream, acting as a carrier for various sex hormones, including estrogen and testosterone. When hormones bind to SHBG, they are temporarily inactive. This test measures the level of this binding protein to help doctors assess overall hormonal balance. Physicians may order this test when evaluating symptoms related to hormone levels or investigating potential issues with thyroid function. Abnormal results can suggest changes in liver function or metabolic conditions that affect how your body manages sex hormones.

short_description: This test measures carboxyhemoglobin in your blood, which shows if hemoglobin has attached to carbon monoxide instead of oxygen.
clinical_overview: The carboxyhemoglobin test quantifies the percentage of hemoglobin—the protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen—that is bound to carbon monoxide (CO). This measurement is typically ordered when a person may have been exposed to high levels of CO, such as from vehicle exhaust or faulty heating sources. Elevated levels indicate significant exposure because carboxyhemoglobin reduces the amount of oxygen available for the body's tissues and organs. The results help healthcare providers assess the severity of carbon monoxide poisoning and determine if immediate treatment is necessary.

short_description: This test measures Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) levels in your blood. FSH is a hormone that helps regulate the reproductive cycle and assess ovarian function.
clinical_overview: Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) is a key hormone produced by the pituitary gland that plays a vital role in regulating egg development and the menstrual cycle. This test measures the concentration of FSH in your blood to help evaluate overall reproductive health and estimate ovarian reserve. Elevated levels may suggest that the ovaries are not functioning as expected, while low levels could indicate issues with the pituitary gland itself. The results must be interpreted by a healthcare provider alongside your age, symptoms, medical history, and other test findings.

short_description: This test measures the level of Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) in your blood. FSH is a hormone that helps regulate reproductive function.
clinical_overview: Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) is a hormone produced by the pituitary gland that plays a key role in regulating the reproductive system, affecting both men and women. This test measures the concentration of FSH circulating in your blood to help evaluate hormonal balance. Doctors typically order this test when investigating potential causes of fertility issues or suspected endocrine imbalances. Elevated or low levels may suggest problems with the ovaries, testes, or the pituitary gland itself. The results are interpreted by a healthcare provider alongside your medical history and physical examination findings.

short_description: This test measures Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH), a hormone that helps regulate egg development. It is used to evaluate reproductive function and ovarian health.
clinical_overview: Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) is a hormone produced by the pituitary gland that plays a key role in stimulating the ovaries and promoting the maturation of eggs. This blood test measures the amount of FSH present in your bloodstream, which helps healthcare providers evaluate reproductive function and assess ovarian reserve. Elevated levels may suggest that the ovaries are not functioning as expected, while low levels can point to issues with the pituitary gland itself. The results must be reviewed by a doctor alongside your medical history and physical exam findings to determine their overall meaning.

short_description: This test measures the amount of Luteinizing Hormone (LH) in your blood. This hormone helps regulate reproductive functions, such as ovulation.
clinical_overview: Luteinizing Hormone (LH) is a pituitary gland hormone that plays a key role in regulating the menstrual cycle and supporting reproductive health. This test measures the concentration of LH in the bloodstream to evaluate how well the endocrine system is functioning. Elevated or low levels may indicate issues with ovarian function, hormonal imbalances, or other conditions affecting reproduction. Results are interpreted by your healthcare provider alongside your symptoms and medical history to help guide diagnosis.

short_description: This test measures the amount of Luteinizing Hormone (LH) in your blood. This hormone helps regulate reproductive functions, such as ovulation.
clinical_overview: The LH 3 SPEC test measures the concentration of Luteinizing Hormone (LH), a key hormone produced by the pituitary gland. Doctors order this test to evaluate how well the endocrine system and reproductive organs are functioning. Elevated or low levels can help determine if there is an issue with hormone production, which may be related to menstrual cycle irregularities or fertility concerns. The results must always be interpreted alongside your medical history, physical exam findings, and other lab tests, as they provide only one piece of information about overall health.

short_description: This test measures Luteinizing Hormone (LH), a hormone that helps regulate reproductive cycles and function. It provides a quantitative measure of this hormone in your blood.
clinical_overview: The LH 4 SPEC test measures the concentration level of Luteinizing Hormone (LH) in the blood. This hormone is produced by the pituitary gland and plays a key role in regulating the menstrual cycle and reproductive health in both men and women. Physicians typically order this test to evaluate potential hormonal imbalances or when investigating causes of irregular periods, infertility, or other issues related to the endocrine system. The measured levels help healthcare providers determine if LH production is within the expected range for your age and sex; however, these results are only one part of a complete evaluation.

short_description: This test measures the amount of carbon dioxide ($ ext{CO}_2$) gas dissolved in your blood. Levels help doctors assess how well your lungs and body are managing acid-base balance.
clinical_overview: This test measures the partial pressure of carbon dioxide ($ ext{PCO}_2$), a key component found in arterial blood. It is used to evaluate the body's overall acid-base status and determine if the respiratory system is functioning correctly. Doctors typically order this when they need to assess how effectively your lungs are removing $ ext{CO}_2$ or if there is an imbalance between acids and bases in the bloodstream. Elevated levels may suggest poor ventilation, while low levels can indicate breathing difficulties. The results help guide treatment for various conditions affecting lung function.

short_description: This blood test measures two forms of Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA)—total and free—to help doctors evaluate the health of the prostate gland.
clinical_overview: Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA) is a protein naturally produced by the prostate gland. This test measures both the total amount and the unbound, or 'free,' portion of this protein in your blood. Doctors order this test to assess potential issues with the prostate, such as inflammation, enlargement (benign prostatic hyperplasia), or cancer. Comparing the ratio of free PSA to total PSA can help doctors determine if an elevated level is more likely due to a benign condition or requires further investigation. The results are used alongside physical exam findings and symptoms to guide medical decisions.

short_description: This test measures autoantibodies—immune proteins that mistakenly target the body's own cells or tissues. Elevated levels may suggest an underlying autoimmune condition.
clinical_overview: The CAR autoantibody test analyzes blood for specific antibodies that have been produced by the immune system against components of the body itself. These 'autoantibodies' indicate that the immune system is incorrectly identifying healthy tissues as foreign invaders. This test is typically ordered to help diagnose or monitor various autoimmune disorders when symptoms are complex or other diagnostic tests are inconclusive. The presence and level of these antibodies can provide information about the activity of an autoimmune process, helping healthcare providers determine if further treatment or management changes are necessary.

short_description: This test measures the level of homocysteine, an amino acid found in your blood. High levels may be associated with increased risk factors for heart and blood vessel problems.
clinical_overview: The Homocysteine test measures the concentration of homocysteine, a type of amino acid, in the blood. This measurement is ordered to help evaluate potential risks related to cardiovascular health, including issues with the heart and blood vessels. Elevated levels of homocysteine are associated with an increased risk profile for developing vascular disease. However, this single result does not diagnose any condition; rather, it helps healthcare providers assess overall risk when combined with a patient's medical history, physical exam findings, and other lab results.

short_description: This test checks for the presence of viable sperm in semen after a vasectomy procedure. It confirms that no active sperm are present, indicating successful completion of the procedure.
clinical_overview: This test measures whether or not viable sperm cells are detectable in a semen sample following a vasectomy. The primary purpose is to confirm the effectiveness of the surgical procedure by verifying that residual sperm count is zero. A negative result indicates that the vasectomy was successful in preventing future sperm release, which is necessary for confirming sterility. It is important to note that this test must be performed several weeks after the procedure and may require follow-up testing to achieve definitive confirmation.

test_name: Salmonella/Shigella Culture, Campy EIA and Shiga Toxin reflex E.coli O157 Culture
short_description: This test detects the presence of specific bacteria, such as Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, and certain strains of E. coli, that can cause stomach infections.
clinical_overview: This panel of tests is used to identify potential bacterial causes when a patient presents with symptoms like diarrhea, fever, or abdominal pain suggestive of gastroenteritis. The test analyzes samples for the presence of specific pathogens, including *Salmonella*, *Shigella*, and *Campylobacter* bacteria, as well as certain strains of *E. coli* that produce Shiga toxin. A positive result indicates that these specific organisms were found in the sample; however, this finding must be interpreted alongside the patient's symptoms and medical history to determine the cause of illness. Negative results do not rule out all causes of diarrhea, which can be caused by viruses or other factors.

short_description: This test measures the amount of ceruloplasmin, a protein in your blood that helps carry copper. Low levels may suggest issues with how your body processes copper.
clinical_overview: Ceruloplasmin is a protein found in the blood that plays a key role in transporting copper throughout the body. This test measures the concentration of this specific protein to help evaluate copper metabolism. Doctors typically order this test when they suspect an underlying disorder affecting copper handling, such as Wilson's disease. Low levels can indicate impaired function or absorption of copper, but these results must be interpreted alongside your symptoms and physical exam findings. The measured value helps guide further diagnostic steps rather than providing a definitive diagnosis alone.

short_description: This test measures the current level of carbamazepine in your blood. It helps doctors ensure that the medication dose is safe and effective for managing conditions like seizures.
clinical_overview: This test quantifies the total amount of carbamazepine, a common anti-seizure medication, circulating in the bloodstream. The primary purpose of this measurement is therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM), which assesses whether the patient's current dosage falls within a safe and effective range. Results are critical for managing conditions such as epilepsy or bipolar disorder. If levels are too low, it may suggest that the dose needs to be increased; conversely, high levels could indicate an overdose risk requiring immediate adjustment of care.

short_description: This test detects materials from *B. tropicalis*, a bacteria that can cause certain tropical illnesses, helping confirm exposure or infection.
clinical_overview: This laboratory test measures specific components of the bacterium *B. tropicalis*. It is ordered to help determine if an individual has been exposed to or infected by this pathogen, which causes various tropical diseases. The results indicate whether these bacterial materials are present in the sample; a positive result suggests potential infection but does not confirm a diagnosis on its own. Physicians interpret these findings alongside your symptoms, medical history, and physical exam results to determine the cause of illness.

short_description: This test measures the amount of chloride, a key salt found in your blood. It helps doctors assess how well your kidneys are managing body fluids and salts.
clinical_overview: Chloride is an essential electrolyte, or mineral salt, that plays a critical role in maintaining the balance of fluids throughout the body. Doctors order this test primarily to evaluate kidney function and overall fluid status. The results help determine if there are imbalances caused by conditions like dehydration, excessive vomiting, or issues with how the kidneys are filtering waste. Abnormal levels may indicate problems with the heart, digestive system, or kidneys, requiring further medical attention.

short_description: This test measures the amount of valproic acid in your blood. Low levels suggest that the medication is either absent or has been metabolized and cleared from the body.
clinical_overview: This test quantifies the concentration of valproic acid, a common medication used to manage conditions such as seizures or mood disorders. It is ordered primarily for therapeutic drug monitoring, which helps healthcare providers ensure that the dosage of the medicine remains within a safe and effective range. The measured results help determine if the current dose is appropriate for your body's needs. Very low levels may suggest that the medication has been cleared from the system or that dosing compliance is an issue, while high levels could indicate toxicity or improper metabolism.

short_description: This test measures specific antibodies in your blood that indicate immunity to Tetanus and Diphtheria, usually due to prior vaccination.
clinical_overview: The Tetanus & Diphtheria IgG test measures the presence of specific antibodies (IgG) in the blood. These antibodies are part of the immune system's memory and confirm if a person has protective immunity against these two serious diseases. This test is ordered to assess immunization status, helping healthcare providers determine if booster shots are needed. High levels of these antibodies suggest adequate protection; however, low or negative results may indicate that vaccination is required. Tetanus can cause painful muscle spasms, while Diphtheria affects the throat and heart.

test_name: Active Chickenpox (Varicella) DNA Test - PCR Blood Test - Varicella-Zoster Virus DNA, Qualitative, Real-Time PCR
short_description: This test detects genetic material (DNA) from the Varicella-Zoster Virus in your blood. A positive result suggests the virus is currently active.
clinical_overview: This laboratory test measures the presence of specific DNA from the Varicella-Zoster Virus directly in a blood sample. It is used to determine if the virus is actively replicating or present in the bloodstream, which helps clinicians evaluate for an acute infection. A positive result indicates that the genetic material of the virus was detected by the specialized testing method. However, this test alone cannot diagnose the illness; results must be interpreted alongside your symptoms and physical examination findings.

short_description: This test checks for the presence of *T. rubrum*, a parasite found in certain body fluids. It detects specific genetic material belonging to this organism.
clinical_overview: The IMCAP T. RUBRUM assay measures the genetic material (DNA) of *Trypanosoma rubrum*, which is a type of parasite that can cause infections. This test is typically ordered when a doctor suspects an infection caused by this specific parasite, often in cases involving chronic or unexplained symptoms. A positive result indicates that the parasite's DNA was found in the sample, suggesting active infection; however, these results must be interpreted alongside your medical history and physical exam findings. The presence of the organism’s material helps guide treatment decisions for managing the condition.

short_description: This test measures key substances in your blood, including electrolytes and markers for kidney and liver function. It helps doctors assess how well your body's basic metabolic processes are working.
clinical_overview: The Basic Metabolic Panel evaluates the concentration of several vital substances in your blood, providing a snapshot of overall bodily function. Markers measured include electrolytes (such as sodium and potassium), which are essential for nerve and muscle signaling, along with indicators of kidney waste removal (creatinine) and blood sugar levels (glucose). Physicians order this test to evaluate metabolic balance when assessing symptoms like fatigue or changes in urination patterns. Abnormal results may suggest issues with the kidneys, liver function, or an imbalance in necessary minerals. These results are always interpreted by a healthcare provider alongside your medical history and physical examination findings.

test_name: Hepatic Function Panel without Total Protein
short_description: This test measures several substances in your blood that help doctors evaluate how well your liver is working. It checks for markers of liver cell damage and the liver's ability to process waste.
clinical_overview: The Hepatic Function Panel without Total Protein evaluates various markers in the blood to assess overall liver health and function. These measurements include enzymes, which can indicate if liver cells are damaged, and proteins, which show the liver’s capacity to perform essential tasks like filtering waste or building necessary substances. Doctors order this test when they need to evaluate potential liver injury, monitor chronic liver conditions, or investigate symptoms such as jaundice (yellowing of the skin). Abnormal results may suggest issues with bile flow, inflammation, or impaired liver synthesis, but these findings must be interpreted alongside your medical history and physical examination.

short_description: This test measures the levels of key minerals, called electrolytes, in your blood, such as sodium and potassium. These minerals are essential for maintaining proper nerve and muscle function.
clinical_overview: The electrolyte panel measures the concentrations of several vital minerals, including sodium, potassium, chloride, and calcium, found in your blood. Electrolytes are critical because they help maintain fluid balance and electrical signals necessary for normal bodily functions. Doctors order this test to evaluate potential imbalances that could affect heart rhythm, nerve function, or muscle contraction. For instance, abnormal levels can signal issues with kidney function, dehydration, or the effects of certain medications. The results assist healthcare providers in monitoring overall mineral status and determining if an underlying medical condition is disrupting the body's electrical balance.

short_description: This test measures free T-3, which is the active form of thyroid hormone circulating in your blood. It helps doctors assess how well your body's metabolism is regulated by this key hormone.
clinical_overview: The Free T-3 test measures the amount of triiodothyronine (T3) that is unbound and actively available to tissues throughout the body. This hormone, produced by the thyroid gland, plays a critical role in regulating your overall metabolic rate. Doctors order this test to evaluate thyroid function and determine if there is an imbalance in how hormones are circulating. Abnormal results may indicate issues with the thyroid gland itself or problems with how the body processes these hormones; for example, low levels can be associated with hypothyroidism (an underactive thyroid). The results must always be interpreted alongside your symptoms, medical history, and physical exam findings.

short_description: This test measures Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH), a hormone that helps regulate egg production in women. Levels can help doctors evaluate reproductive health.
clinical_overview: The FSH test measures the concentration of Follicle-Stimulating Hormone in your blood. This hormone is part of the body's system that controls the development and release of eggs, making it a key marker for evaluating overall reproductive function. Doctors typically order this test to assess ovarian reserve or when investigating potential causes of infertility. Elevated or low levels may suggest issues with either the ovaries themselves or the pituitary gland, but these results must always be interpreted by your healthcare provider alongside your medical history and physical exam findings.

short_description: This test measures Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH), a hormone that helps regulate reproductive function. It provides information about the health of your ovaries or testes.
clinical_overview: Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) is a pituitary gland hormone essential for regulating the reproductive system in both men and women. This blood test measures the concentration of FSH to evaluate overall reproductive endocrine function. Doctors typically order this test when assessing ovarian reserve, investigating menstrual cycle irregularities, or evaluating fertility concerns. Elevated or low levels may suggest issues with the ovaries, testes, or the pituitary gland itself. The results are interpreted by a healthcare provider alongside your medical history and physical exam findings.

short_description: This test measures Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH), a hormone in the blood that helps regulate reproductive functions, such as egg development.
clinical_overview: The FSH test measures the concentration of Follicle-Stimulating Hormone in the bloodstream. This hormone is released by the pituitary gland and plays a key role in regulating the menstrual cycle and supporting the maturation of eggs. Physicians typically order this test to evaluate reproductive health, especially when investigating potential causes of infertility or assessing ovarian function. Elevated FSH levels may suggest that the ovaries are not functioning as expected, while very low levels could indicate issues with the pituitary gland itself. The results help guide further testing and treatment plans.

short_description: This test measures Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH), a hormone that helps regulate reproductive function. Levels can help doctors evaluate ovarian health and overall fertility.
clinical_overview: The FSH test measures the concentration of Follicle-Stimulating Hormone in the blood. This hormone is part of the body's system that controls reproductive organs, making this test useful for evaluating overall reproductive health. Doctors typically order this test to assess ovarian function or when investigating potential causes of difficulty conceiving. Elevated levels may suggest lower ovarian reserve, while other patterns can help pinpoint if the issue lies with the ovaries or another gland. The results are used by healthcare providers in conjunction with a patient's medical history and physical exam findings.

short_description: This test measures the level of Luteinizing Hormone (LH) in your blood. LH is a key hormone involved in regulating menstrual cycles and overall reproductive health.
clinical_overview: Luteinizing Hormone (LH) is a pituitary hormone that plays a critical role in regulating the reproductive system, stimulating hormones necessary for ovulation and maintaining normal menstrual cycles. This test measures the concentration of LH in your blood to help evaluate hormonal balance. Doctors typically order this test when investigating irregular periods, fertility concerns, or suspected endocrine imbalances. The results are interpreted by comparing your levels to established reference ranges; abnormal findings may suggest conditions affecting the ovaries, pituitary gland, or other reproductive organs.

short_description: This test measures Luteinizing Hormone (LH), a hormone that helps regulate reproductive cycles and function by stimulating the ovaries or testes.
clinical_overview: Luteinizing Hormone (LH) is a key hormone released into the bloodstream that signals the reproductive organs, such as the ovaries or testes, to perform their functions. Doctors order this test primarily to evaluate hormonal balance and assess potential issues with fertility or menstrual cycle regularity. The results help determine if the pituitary gland—the body's master regulator of hormones—is functioning correctly. Elevated or low levels may suggest underlying conditions affecting hormone production or reproductive health, guiding further diagnosis and treatment plans.

short_description: This test measures Luteinizing Hormone (LH) levels in your blood. LH is a hormone that helps regulate reproductive cycles and pituitary gland function.
clinical_overview: Luteinizing Hormone (LH) is a key hormone produced by the pituitary gland that plays a major role in regulating the menstrual cycle and overall reproductive health. This test measures the concentration of LH in your blood to help doctors evaluate how well the endocrine system is functioning. Physicians typically order this test when investigating irregular periods, fertility concerns, or suspected hormonal imbalances. The results are interpreted alongside other symptoms, medical history, and physical exam findings to determine if hormone levels fall within a normal range for reproductive function.

short_description: This test measures Luteinizing Hormone (LH), a hormone that helps regulate reproductive cycles and pituitary gland function.
clinical_overview: Luteinizing Hormone (LH) is a key hormone released by the pituitary gland that plays a major role in regulating the menstrual cycle and supporting reproductive health. This blood test measures the concentration of LH to help evaluate how well the endocrine system, particularly the pituitary gland, is functioning. Doctors typically order this test when investigating irregular periods, fertility concerns, or suspected issues with hormonal balance. Elevated or low levels may suggest underlying problems affecting the ovaries, testes, or the glands that control hormone release. The results are interpreted by a healthcare provider alongside your symptoms and medical history.

short_description: This test measures the level of Lipoprotein(a) in your blood. High levels indicate a protein associated with fats that may increase the risk of heart and blood vessel issues.
clinical_overview: Lipoprotein(a) is a specific type of protein found in the blood that contributes to plaque buildup within the arteries. This test measures the concentration of this protein, which is important because elevated levels are linked to an increased risk of developing cardiovascular disease, such as heart attack and stroke. The result helps your healthcare provider assess your overall risk profile when combined with factors like family history and other cholesterol measurements. It is used for evaluating long-term cardiovascular risk rather than diagnosing a specific acute event.

short_description: Measures Troponin I, a protein found in heart muscle cells. Elevated levels in the blood suggest damage or stress to the heart muscle.
clinical_overview: Troponin I is a specific protein located within the heart muscle. When the heart muscle experiences injury or significant stress, this protein leaks into the bloodstream. This test measures the amount of Troponin I present in the blood, providing an indicator of potential damage to the cardiac tissue. Physicians typically order this test when a patient presents with symptoms like chest pain, as it helps evaluate whether the heart muscle has been damaged. Elevated levels suggest that injury has occurred and require further medical evaluation; however, these results must always be interpreted alongside the patient's symptoms, physical exam findings, and medical history.

short_description: This test detects specific proteins (antigens) from *Helicobacter pylori*, a common bacteria that can cause stomach irritation, in your stool sample.
clinical_overview: The purpose of this test is to detect the presence of antigens—specific components—from *Helicobacter pylori* bacteria shed into the stool. This procedure helps healthcare providers determine if an individual has an active infection with this bacterium. While many people carry *H. pylori*, detecting its antigens in the stool can help diagnose conditions like gastritis or peptic ulcers, which are inflammation or sores in the stomach lining. A positive result suggests current colonization by the bacteria and may prompt further treatment to reduce the risk of complications.

short_description: This test measures antibodies that target the GAD-65 protein, an enzyme found in nerve cells. Detecting these specific antibodies can help evaluate for autoimmune conditions.
clinical_overview: The GAD-65 antibody test detects autoantibodies—proteins made by the immune system that mistakenly attack the body's own tissues—specifically those targeting the GAD-65 enzyme. This test is typically ordered to help investigate potential autoimmune disorders, such as certain types of encephalomyelitis or type 1 diabetes, when symptoms suggest an underlying problem with the nervous system. A positive result indicates that these specific antibodies are present in the blood, suggesting an immune reaction against the body's own cells. However, a positive test result does not confirm a diagnosis on its own and must be interpreted by a healthcare provider alongside your medical history, physical exam findings, and other lab results.

short_description: This test measures estriol, a type of estrogen hormone found in the blood. It helps assess overall reproductive hormone levels.
clinical_overview: Estriol is an estrogen hormone that plays a role in regulating reproductive health and is measured in the serum (blood). This test is ordered to evaluate hormonal balance, particularly when assessing ovarian function or monitoring pregnancy status. The level of estriol can provide information about how well the body's endocrine system is functioning at that time. While results help healthcare providers understand hormone patterns, they are used only as part of a complete evaluation and cannot diagnose a specific condition on their own.

short_description: This test examines your DNA for a specific genetic marker, IMPAC WALNUT (F256). It determines if certain variations are present in the sample.
clinical_overview: This molecular diagnostic test analyzes genetic material from the provided sample to detect the presence of a specific biomarker known as IMPAC WALNUT (F256). Physicians order this test when they need to evaluate potential risk factors or confirm a diagnosis based on established genetic patterns. A positive result indicates that the specific marker is detectable, which can help guide treatment decisions for certain conditions. However, these results must always be interpreted by your healthcare provider in conjunction with your full medical history and physical examination findings.

short_description: This test checks for antibodies in your blood that cause red blood cells to clump together when cooled. It helps detect specific immune responses against certain pathogens.
clinical_overview: The COLD HEMAGGLUTININS test measures specific antibodies present in the blood that react with and cause red blood cells to clump (agglutinate) at cooler temperatures. This type of testing is ordered by healthcare providers to help evaluate potential infections or determine if the immune system has mounted a response against certain pathogens. A positive result suggests the presence of these antibodies, indicating an immune reaction; however, this finding must always be interpreted alongside your symptoms and medical history. These results are used as part of a diagnostic process, not as a standalone diagnosis.

short_description: This test measures blood glucose levels over time after consuming a measured amount of sugar. It helps determine how well your body processes and manages sugar.
clinical_overview: The Glucose Tolerance Test (GTT) assesses the body's ability to manage blood sugar, or glucose, after ingesting a standardized sugary drink. When ordered, this test evaluates whether the pancreas and insulin system are working correctly to keep glucose levels stable. Elevated readings may indicate impaired glucose tolerance or diabetes mellitus, conditions where the body struggles to process sugar efficiently. Results help healthcare providers determine if lifestyle changes or further medical management is needed.

short_description: This test measures the level of intact Parathyroid Hormone (PTH) in your blood. PTH is a hormone that helps regulate calcium levels throughout the body.
clinical_overview: Parathyroid Hormone (PTH) is a key hormone responsible for maintaining stable calcium levels in the blood, a process called calcium homeostasis. This quantitative test measures the amount of intact PTH circulating in the bloodstream. It is typically ordered when a patient presents with symptoms or lab results suggesting abnormal calcium levels (either too high or too low). The measured level helps healthcare providers evaluate if the parathyroid glands are functioning correctly. Abnormal results may indicate conditions such as primary hyperparathyroidism, which involves the overproduction of PTH.

short_description: This evaluation checks the structure and amount of hemoglobin in your blood to identify potential disorders affecting red blood cells.
clinical_overview: Hemoglobinopathy refers to a group of conditions that affect the structure or quantity of hemoglobin, the protein inside red blood cells responsible for carrying oxygen. This diagnostic panel is ordered when a patient shows signs of anemia (low red blood cell count) or when physical examination and other lab results suggest an abnormality in how red blood cells are formed. The testing evaluates both the overall number of red blood cells and the specific proteins they contain, helping to identify underlying conditions such as sickle cell disease or thalassemia. These findings help healthcare providers determine the cause of oxygen-carrying issues and guide appropriate treatment.

short_description: This test measures the amount of Hepatitis C Virus genetic material (RNA) in your blood. It helps doctors determine if the virus is currently active or how well a treatment is working.
clinical_overview: The HCV RNA test detects specific pieces of genetic material from the Hepatitis C Virus within the bloodstream. Because it measures the actual viral load, this test is used to confirm an active infection and monitor whether a person’s body is responding to antiviral treatments. High levels of detectable RNA suggest that the virus is actively replicating in the body; conversely, low or undetectable levels often indicate successful treatment or clearance of the virus. This information helps healthcare providers manage the condition and determine if further medical intervention is needed.

short_description: This test detects if your red blood cells are coated with antibodies. A positive result suggests your immune system may be attacking your own red blood cells.
clinical_overview: The Coombs Direct Test measures whether foreign or self-antibodies (autoantibodies) are attached to the surface of your red blood cells. This test is typically ordered when a patient has unexplained anemia, meaning they have low red blood cell counts without an obvious cause. A positive result indicates that antibodies are coating the red blood cells, suggesting that the immune system is mistakenly attacking them. While this finding points toward an autoimmune process—such as autoimmune hemolytic anemia—it does not confirm a diagnosis on its own and requires correlation with symptoms, medical history, and other lab results.

short_description: This test measures antibodies in your blood that may indicate exposure to syphilis, a sexually transmitted infection.
clinical_overview: This panel of tests evaluates for specific antibodies related to syphilis. Syphilis is an infection caused by bacteria and can affect various parts of the body if untreated.
